Quantity of interest:
- Name: Income elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment for prime-age working US workers
runs.reasoningSummary: Standard economic consensus from structural models and micro data finds small positive intensive margin elasticity for prime-age males (around 0.1), with somewhat higher for females; income effect offsets substitution weakly; excludes participation.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment for prime-age working US workers
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates for US prime-age males show small positive intensive margin response to non-labor income; females slightly higher but overall low due to income effects dominating substitution. Frisch elasticity bounds around 0.1-0.3 conditional on working.
